title: The Time-Varying Price of Financial Intermediation in the Mortgage Market

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This staff report examines the cost of financial intermediation in the U.S. mortgage market, finding an average price of 142 basis points over the 2008-14 period. The research reveals that while price changes in secondary markets pass through to borrowers almost completely at daily frequencies, monthly intermediation prices fluctuate significantly and are highly sensitive to volume, with a one standard deviation increase in applications raising the price by 30-35 basis points. The authors estimate total implicit costs to borrowers of approximately $135 billion over this period and find that quantitative easing increased application volumes, which in turn raised intermediation costs and attenuated QE benefits to borrowers.
key_points: Average price of mortgage market intermediation was 142 basis points over 2008-14
At daily frequencies, secondary market price changes pass through to primary market borrowers almost completely
A one standard deviation increase in mortgage applications leads to a 30-35 basis point increase in the price of intermediation
The price of intermediation increased by approximately 30 basis points per year over 2008-14
The trend likely reflects higher mortgage servicing costs and increased legal and regulatory burden
Quantitative easing, by increasing application volumes, substantially increased the price of intermediation, attenuating QE benefits to borrowers
Estimated total implicit cost to borrowers of approximately $135 billion over 2008-14 period
why_it_matters: This research quantifies how much of the flow from savers to borrowers is captured by intermediaries in the mortgage market. Understanding intermediation costs and their drivers—including regulatory burden and capacity constraints—is essential for assessing policy effectiveness (e.g., QE transmission) and market efficiency. The finding that QE attenuates benefits through higher intermediation costs during high-volume periods has direct implications for monetary policy design.
market_structure_context: The paper examines market structure through the lens of intermediation costs in the mortgage market, a key segment of fixed-income markets. It documents the pass-through mechanism from secondary to primary markets and identifies capacity constraints and regulatory burden as structural drivers of intermediation pricing. QE is shown to affect market structure by creating volume spikes that tighten intermediation capacity.
important_numbers: 30-35 basis points (price increase per standard deviation application increase)

issues_found: Published_at in record is '2017-01-01' but source metadata shows original publication in January 2017 with revision in August 2017. The August 2017 date should be used as it represents the final version.
Source text is an abstract/summary only. Full methodology, data details, and policy implications cannot be verified against the complete paper.
The source explicitly states 'Available only in PDF', limiting verification depth.

human_review.notes: Record is well-constructed and accurate to the available source material, but is limited to abstract-only content. The August 2017 publication date should be corrected. Human review needed to assess whether historical research from 2017 on mortgage intermediation costs remains relevant to current market structure discussions, and whether the record's policy implications (QE attenuation) are appropriately calibrated.

notes: Source is a New York Fed staff report (research paper) examining mortgage market intermediation costs. Directly relevant to market structure. Publication date of January 2017 (revised August 2017) makes this a historical record—approximately 9 years old as of TODAY (2026-03-31). The content provides valuable structural insights about how intermediation costs function in mortgage markets, including QE spillover effects on market structure. Lowered confidence slightly due to age. Available only in PDF per source disclosure.
